name: Dead Sea
description: The Dead Sea, also known as the Salt Sea, is a salt lake bordered by Jordan to the east and Israel and the West Bank to the west. It is one of the world's saltiest bodies of water, with a salt concentration significantly higher than that of the ocean. This extreme salinity makes it inhospitable to most life forms, hence its name. In Middle Eastern mythology and religious traditions, particularly within Judeo-Christian narratives, the Dead Sea is most famously associated with the destruction of Sodom and Gomorrah. These cities, known for their wickedness, were said to have been destroyed by God with fire and brimstone, and the Dead Sea now occupies the area where they were supposedly located. The sea is thus viewed as a symbol of divine punishment and the consequences of sin. Its desolate landscape and unique geological features contribute to its mystique and reinforce its association with destruction and judgment. The Dead Sea also holds significance in Jewish tradition, with some believing it possesses healing properties. The high mineral content of the water and mud is thought to have therapeutic benefits, leading to its modern-day use in health and beauty treatments. The inhospitable environment and the biblical stories surrounding it have cemented the Dead Sea's place as a powerful and enduring symbol in Middle Eastern mythology and culture, representing both destruction and potential for renewal.
subtitle: Sea of Salt, Symbol of Destruction
associatedDeities: Yahweh/God (associated with the destruction of Sodom and Gomorrah)

keyMyths.title: Destruction of Sodom and Gomorrah
keyMyths.description: God destroyed the cities of Sodom and Gomorrah with fire and brimstone due to their wickedness. The Dead Sea now occupies the area where these cities were believed to be located, serving as a reminder of divine judgment.

keyMyths.title: Lot's Wife
keyMyths.description: During the escape from Sodom, Lot's wife disobeyed the angels' command and looked back at the city, and she was immediately turned into a pillar of salt. Some believe this pillar stood near the Dead Sea.

sources.author: Genesis
sources.description: The primary source for the story of Sodom and Gomorrah and Lot's wife.
sources.text: The primary source for the story of Sodom and Gomorrah and Lot's wife.

sources.author: Josephus
sources.description: Historical account referencing the Dead Sea and the region's history.
